operating_state: Total net sales increased 2% to $119.6 billion driven by iPhone growth of 6% and Services expansion of 11%, partially offset by iPad decline of 25% and Wearables decrease of 11%. Regional performance showed strength in Europe and Japan with double-digit growth, while Greater China declined 13% due to lower iPhone and iPad sales. The quarter included 13 weeks compared to 14 weeks in the prior year period.
cost_structure: Total gross margin percentage expanded to 45.9% from 43.0% driven by cost savings and favorable product mix. Products gross margin improved to 39.4% from 37.0% due to cost savings and mix benefits, partially offset by foreign currency headwinds. Services gross margin increased to 72.8% from 70.8% driven by different Services mix. Operating expenses remained flat at 12% of net sales.
material_events: The company repurchased $20.5 billion of common stock and paid $3.8 billion in dividends during the quarter. Updated MacBook Pro and iMac were announced. Annual shareholder meeting held February 28, 2024 with all director nominees elected and executive compensation approved, while five shareholder proposals were rejected.
macro_exposures: Foreign currency fluctuations had mixed regional impacts with strength in foreign currencies benefiting Americas and Europe while renminbi weakness hurt Greater China performance and yen weakness affected Japan despite local currency growth. Manufacturing purchase obligations totaled $38.0 billion with substantially all payable within 12 months.
forward_outlook: The company maintains its intention to increase dividends annually subject to Board approval, with current quarterly dividend at $0.24 per share. Management expects gross margins to remain subject to volatility and downward pressure. The company believes current cash balances, operational cash generation and debt market access will satisfy cash requirements and capital return programs over the next 12 months and beyond.
uncertainty_profile: Regulatory scrutiny intensified with January 2024 announcement of iOS, App Store and Safari changes in the European Union to comply with the Digital Markets Act, though compliance plan remains subject to potential challenge. Ongoing antitrust investigations in multiple jurisdictions could result in significant fines and required business changes. Commercial relationships with technology partners face risk from investigations and litigation that could affect licensing arrangements, including search services revenue.
